1: Psalm 36 is unusual in its structure: a wisdom meditation on wickedness (Psalms 36:1–4) followed immediately by a hymn to divine character (Psalms 36:5–9) and a brief petition with confidence statement (Psalms 36:10–12). The superscription calls it a psalm of "David the servant of the LORD" — the ʿebed YHWH (servant of the LORD) designation links the author to the great servant figures of the prophets. The opening is remarkable: "the voice of transgression (nĕʾum-pešaʿ) speaks deep within the heart of the wicked." Nĕʾum is the prophetic oracle formula ("oracle of the LORD," nĕʾum YHWH); here wickedness itself speaks with prophetic authority from within the wicked person's lēb (heart). Sin is personified as an inner oracle, a demonic voice dictating the wicked's inner life.
2: "For he deceives himself into thinking his guilt cannot be discovered and condemned (limsōʾ ʿăwōnô lĕśānōʾ)" — the self-deception of the wicked is the theme. Hĕlîq (hāllaq, to be smooth, flattering) — the wicked person speaks smoothly to himself, self-flattering inner speech that makes his own guilt invisible. The result: there is no "fear of God before his eyes" — quoted by Paul in Romans 3:18 as the defining statement of universal human sinfulness and the culmination of his catena of OT texts demonstrating universal guilt (Romans 3:10–18). In Paul's reading, this is not only the condition of the wicked but the condition of all humans apart from divine grace.
3: "His mouth pours out wickedness (ʾāwen) and deception (mirmāh)" — ʾāwen (wickedness, vanity, emptiness, idolatry) is one of the Psalter's key moral terms, combining the meanings of wickedness, emptiness, and often idolatrous practice. The coupling of ʾāwen with mirmāh (treachery, deception) describes speech that is both morally corrupt and strategically deceptive. "He has stopped acting wisely (ḥādal lĕhaśkîl)" — śākal (to act wisely, have insight, prosper) is the wisdom vocabulary; to cease from wisdom is to abandon the fundamental orientation of covenant life. This cessation is presented as a deliberate act, not merely ignorance.
4: "On his bed he hatches schemes (yĕḥaššōb ʾāwen)" — the privacy of the bed where no social constraint operates becomes the laboratory of wickedness. The bed as the place of wicked scheming also appears in Micah 2:1: "Woe to those who plan iniquity and devise evil on their beds." "He places himself on a path that is not good (ʿal-derek lōʾ-ṭôb yityaṣṣēb)" — the deliberate positioning on an evil path; yāṣab (to stand firm, position oneself) suggests intentional commitment, not accidental wandering. "He refuses to reject evil (rāʿ lōʾ yimʾās)" — the active refusal to turn from evil. The portrait of wickedness in Psalms 36:1–4 is complete: inner oracle, self-deception, wicked speech, cessation of wisdom, nocturnal scheming, deliberate path-setting, refusal of repentance.
5: "Your steadfast love (ḥasdĕkā), LORD, reaches to the heavens (bašāmayim); your faithfulness (ʾĕmûnātĕkā) extends to the clouds" — after the claustrophobic portrait of wickedness (Psalms 36:1–4), the psalm explodes into cosmic scope. The divine attributes are mapped onto the vertical dimensions of the cosmos: ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) reaches to the heavens; ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability, steadiness) extends to the clouds. The heavens and clouds together represent the upper atmospheric sphere — but also beyond it: the attributes of God transcend even the highest created reality.
6: "Your righteousness (ṣidqātĕkā) towers like the mighty mountains (harĕrê-ʾēl); your judgments (mišpāṭêkā) plunge as deep as the ocean (tĕhôm rabbāh)" — the four paired terms (ḥesed, ʾĕmûnāh, ṣĕdāqāh, mišpāṭ) constitute the core vocabulary of divine character in the wisdom and prophetic traditions. Harĕrê-ʾēl ("mountains of God/El") — the superlative use of the divine name ʾEl means "towering/mighty mountains," not mountains literally belonging to God. Tĕhôm rabbāh (the great deep) — the primordial ocean-abyss of Genesis 1:2, Job 38:16, the symbol of immeasurable depth. "You preserve both human and animal (ʾādām ûbĕhēmāh)" — the scope of divine care encompasses all living creatures, both human and non-human (echoing Ps 104 and anticipating the Noahic covenant).
7: "How priceless (mah-yāqār) is your steadfast love, O God! The children of humanity (bĕnê ʾādām) take refuge under the shadow of your wings (bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā)" — the wing-shadow image occurs throughout the Psalter (Psalms 17:8; Psalms 57:1; Psalms 61:4; Psalms 63:7; Psalms 91:4) and in the Torah (Deuteronomy 32:11 — God as eagle bearing Israel on his wings). The image draws on multiple registers: (a) the cherubim wings spread over the ark in the Holy of Holies, representing divine protection; (b) the maternal bird covering her chicks; (c) the Boaz-Ruth idiom of covenant protection (Ruth 2:12: "under whose wings you have come to take refuge"). The temple context may be primary: the ark-cherubim wings were the visible symbol of divine shelter in the sanctuary.
8: "They feast on the richness of your house (yirwĕyûn middeśen bêtêkā)" — the language of the temple feast is explicit: dešen (fatness, richness) is the abundance of the peace offering meal. In the temple liturgy, the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) was the only sacrifice where the worshipper ate — portions of the cooked meat were eaten by the priest, the Levites, and the worshipper's household in the temple courts. To eat from the "fatness of your house" is to participate in this communal sacred meal. "The river of your pleasures (naḥal ʿădānêkā)" — ʿeden (pleasure, delight, Eden); the "river of delights" evokes the rivers of Paradise (Genesis 2:10–14; cf. Ezekiel 47:1–12; Revelation 22:1–2). The temple is the locus of Edenic life restored.
9: "For with you is the fountain of life (mĕqôr ḥayyîm); by your light we see light (bĕʾôrĕkā nirʾeh-ʾôr)" — two of the most theologically dense phrases in the Psalter. Mĕqôr ḥayyîm (fountain/spring of life) — the life that springs from God himself; not merely physical life but the fullness of existence, all goodness and blessing. In Jeremiah's polemic against idolatry, YHWH is "the fountain of living waters" that Israel has abandoned in favor of broken cisterns (Jeremiah 2:13; Psalms 17:13). "By your light we see light" — the divine light that makes all seeing possible; not just one light source among others but the light that enables all perception. John 1:4–9 develops this into the theology of the Logos as the light that enlightens every person.
10: "Extend (mĕšōk) your steadfast love to those who know you (lĕyōdĕʿêkā)" — the petition that follows the meditation: the cosmic character of divine love (Psalms 36:5–9) must reach the specific covenant community. Māšak (draw out, extend, prolong) — the same verb used in the Song of Songs for the king drawing the beloved toward him (Song of Solomon 1:4). "Those who know you" is the covenant insider designation — not intellectual knowledge but relational, covenantal knowing (Genesis 4:1; Hosea 2:20; Psalms 4:1–6; John 17:3). "Your righteousness to the upright in heart (lĕyišrê-lēb)" — the upright of heart are those whose inner orientation aligns with the covenant order.
11: "Let not the foot of arrogance (regle gaʾăwāh) trample me (tĕbōʾēnî), nor the hand of the wicked drive me from my place" — the petition uses two concrete physical images: the trampling foot and the driving hand. In ANE warfare, the king stood with his foot on the neck of the defeated enemy (Joshua 10:24; Psalms 110:1) as the physical symbol of total subjugation. The arrogant foot that tramples is the foot of the conqueror. "My place" (tĕnîdēnî) — being "driven from one's place" is being expelled from land, community, and covenant status.
12: "There the evildoers lie fallen; they have been thrust down and cannot get up" — the prophetic-perfect tense: the fall of the wicked is narrated as already accomplished, the certainty of divine justice so complete that it is spoken in the past tense. "Cannot rise" (yākĕlû qûm) — the permanent, irreversible nature of the fall. The final verse closes the psalm on the note of eschatological confidence: the vast wickedness portrayed in Psalms 36:1–4 and the cosmic goodness portrayed in Psalms 36:5–9 resolve in the assurance that the wicked will fall and the righteous who take refuge in YHWH's love (Psalms 36:7) will be sustained. The structure of the psalm — wickedness / divine character / petition / prophetic confidence — is itself a theological argument for trusting the God whose love exceeds the heavens.
